SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(3): The paper provides theoretical results (closure under intersection, an Earley-like linear-time parser for RGGs) and evaluates on multiple corpora (AMR, Prague Czech-English, UCCA), but the empirical section lacks key details: no baselines, no evaluation metrics, no reported hyperparameters, and code is not mentioned, limiting support for practical claims. No ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported to probe the impact of the RGG constraints (C1/C2) or parsing assumptions. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the authors discuss limitations (expressivity concerns, marker-node assumption). Overall, the work is theoretically solid but the experimental/empirical evaluation is insufficiently detailed to fully substantiate practical claims. | B(2): The experimental evaluation is shallow: although three datasets (AMR, Prague Czech-English, UCCA) are mentioned, no baselines, evaluation metrics, or code/hyperparameters are reported, so the empirical claims are not sufficiently supported. No ablation studies or sensitivity analyses are provided despite algorithmic choices (e.g., constraints C1/C2, ordering) that would merit such analysis. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and limitations (e.g., expressivity issues of RGG, external node assumption) are discussed, but reproducibility is limited by missing implementation details and evaluations.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides theoretical results (closure under intersection, an Earley-like linear-time parser for RGGs) and evaluates on multiple corpora (AMR, Prague Czech-English, UCCA), but the empirical section lacks key details: no baselines, no evaluation metrics, no reported hyperparameters, and code is not mentioned, limiting support for practical claims. No ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported to probe the impact of the RGG constraints (C1/C2) or parsing assumptions. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the authors discuss limitations (expressivity concerns, marker-node assumption). Overall, the work is theoretically solid but the experimental/empirical evaluation is insufficiently detailed to fully substantiate practical claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental evaluation is shallow: although three datasets (AMR, Prague Czech-English, UCCA) are mentioned, no baselines, evaluation metrics, or code/hyperparameters are reported, so the empirical claims are not sufficiently supported. No ablation studies or sensitivity analyses are provided despite algorithmic choices (e.g., constraints C1/C2, ordering) that would merit such analysis. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and limitations (e.g., expressivity issues of RGG, external node assumption) are discussed, but reproducibility is limited by missing implementation details and evaluations.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces Regular Graph Grammars (RGG) as a constrained subclass of hyperedge replacement grammars (C1/C2) and leverages this to define Probabilistic Regular Graph Languages, which is a clear new formalization connecting HRG and MSOL. It also proves nontrivial properties (closure under intersection) and presents an Earley-like top-down parser with linear-time complexity on input graphs—techniques that go beyond straightforward reuse of existing HRG/MSOL results. The authors explicitly position these contributions relative to prior work (RGL as a subfamily of HRL and MSOL) and the combination of constraints plus the efficient parsing algorithm would likely be informative to experts, so the novelty is notable though not necessarily a paradigm shift. | B(4): The paper proposes a distinct class (Regular Graph Grammars with constraints C1/C2) and shows formal properties — e.g., that Regular Graph Languages sit as a subfamily of both HRL and MSOL and are closed under intersection — which is a nontrivial theoretical contribution. The development of a top-down, Earley-like parser with linear runtime for RGGs and the use of boundary representations to achieve more efficient parsing than general HRG further differentiate the work from prior methods. The stated limitations (potentially unnatural grammars and reduced expressivity under C2) temper the contribution but do not negate the clear methodological and formal novelties presented.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper introduces Regular Graph Grammars (RGG) as a constrained subclass of hyperedge replacement grammars (C1/C2) and leverages this to define Probabilistic Regular Graph Languages, which is a clear new formalization connecting HRG and MSOL. It also proves nontrivial properties (closure under intersection) and presents an Earley-like top-down parser with linear-time complexity on input graphs—techniques that go beyond straightforward reuse of existing HRG/MSOL results. The authors explicitly position these contributions relative to prior work (RGL as a subfamily of HRL and MSOL) and the combination of constraints plus the efficient parsing algorithm would likely be informative to experts, so the novelty is notable though not necessarily a paradigm shift.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a distinct class (Regular Graph Grammars with constraints C1/C2) and shows formal properties — e.g., that Regular Graph Languages sit as a subfamily of both HRL and MSOL and are closed under intersection — which is a nontrivial theoretical contribution. The development of a top-down, Earley-like parser with linear runtime for RGGs and the use of boundary representations to achieve more efficient parsing than general HRG further differentiate the work from prior methods. The stated limitations (potentially unnatural grammars and reduced expressivity under C2) temper the contribution but do not negate the clear methodological and formal novelties presented.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized and the main contributions (RGG definition with C1/C2, closure under intersection, and the Earley-like linear-time parser) are clearly stated early; writing quality is reported as good and notation appears consistent. Related work coverage is thorough and examples/figures generally help, but one example grammar (Table 1) is noted as "unnatural" and constraint C2 makes some derivations hard to express, which slightly weakens presentation. The empirical section is unclear: datasets are listed but there are no baselines or evaluation metrics reported, making the experimental presentation incomplete. Overall the paper communicates ideas clearly with minor presentation issues, especially in the empirical reporting and one awkward example. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (definitions of RGG with C1/C2, closure under intersection, and an Earley-like linear-time parser) are stated early and developed logically. Notation and methodology are presented clearly and writing quality is reported as good, with thorough related-work coverage. Figures/tables and examples are generally effective, though one grammar example (Table 1) is noted as "unnatural" and the C2 constraint makes some derivation orders awkward to express, which are minor presentation issues that could be clarified. The mild parser assumption (identifiable external node) is mentioned but should be explicitly highlighted to avoid ambiguity for some applications.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized and the main contributions (RGG definition with C1/C2, closure under intersection, and the Earley-like linear-time parser) are clearly stated early; writing quality is reported as good and notation appears consistent. Related work coverage is thorough and examples/figures generally help, but one example grammar (Table 1) is noted as "unnatural" and constraint C2 makes some derivations hard to express, which slightly weakens presentation. The empirical section is unclear: datasets are listed but there are no baselines or evaluation metrics reported, making the experimental presentation incomplete. Overall the paper communicates ideas clearly with minor presentation issues, especially in the empirical reporting and one awkward example.

Notation and methodology are presented clearly and writing quality is reported as good, with thorough related-work coverage. Figures/tables and examples are generally effective, though one grammar example (Table 1) is noted as "unnatural" and the C2 constraint makes some derivation orders awkward to express, which are minor presentation issues that could be clarified. The mild parser assumption (identifiable external node) is mentioned but should be explicitly highlighted to avoid ambiguity for some applications.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper addresses a topic relevant to NLP—graph grammars and parsing for structured semantic representations (AMR, UCCA, Prague treebank) and makes concrete claims (RGG as a restricted HRG/MSOL subclass, closure under intersection, and an Earley-like linear-time top-down parser). However, its focus is largely formal/theoretical (constraints C1/C2, boundary orderings) with no reported baselines, evaluation metrics, or empirical comparisons, and it admits potential expressivity limitations, which weakens immediate impact for a general ML/NLP audience. Overall this is borderline appropriate: it could fit a specialized NLP/formal-language track but is less clearly a strong match for a mainstream top NLP/ML conference without more empirical validation or demonstrations of practical gains. | B(4): The paper addresses a topic relevant to top NLP/ML venues: graph grammars and efficient parsing for semantic graph formalisms (AMR, UCCA, UD) and proposes a linear-time Earley-like parser and MSOL-compatible grammar class (RGG). It speaks to the right audience (semantic parsing, structured prediction, formal methods in NLP) and formulates the problem in a way the community cares about. However, the work is rather heavy on formal-language theory and lacks empirical baselines, evaluation metrics, and code, which are commonly expected at top ML/NLP conferences—hence a good but not perfect fit.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a topic relevant to NLP—graph grammars and parsing for structured semantic representations (AMR, UCCA, Prague treebank) and makes concrete claims (RGG as a restricted HRG/MSOL subclass, closure under intersection, and an Earley-like linear-time top-down parser). However, its focus is largely formal/theoretical (constraints C1/C2, boundary orderings) with no reported baselines, evaluation metrics, or empirical comparisons, and it admits potential expressivity limitations, which weakens immediate impact for a general ML/NLP audience. Overall this is borderline appropriate: it could fit a specialized NLP/formal-language track but is less clearly a strong match for a mainstream top NLP/ML conference without more empirical validation or demonstrations of practical gains.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses a topic relevant to top NLP/ML venues: graph grammars and efficient parsing for semantic graph formalisms (AMR, UCCA, UD) and proposes a linear-time Earley-like parser and MSOL-compatible grammar class (RGG). It speaks to the right audience (semantic parsing, structured prediction, formal methods in NLP) and formulates the problem in a way the community cares about. However, the work is rather heavy on formal-language theory and lacks empirical baselines, evaluation metrics, and code, which are commonly expected at top ML/NLP conferences—hence a good but not perfect fit.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): The paper presents no empirical baselines ("Baselines compared: none mentioned") and provides no evaluation metrics, so it lacks meaningful empirical comparisons against appropriate recent methods despite using datasets like AMR and UCCA. It does, however, give thorough related-work coverage and theoretical comparisons (e.g., RGL as a subfamily of HRL/MSOL and complexity/expressivity tradeoffs), but the absence of empirical baselines or fair experimental evaluation makes the comparison effort inadequate. | B(2): The paper reports no baseline comparisons (Baselines compared: none mentioned) and provides no evaluation metrics, so it fails to empirically compare the proposed RGG parsing approach against recent methods. While the related work coverage is reported as thorough and the paper discusses theoretical relationships to HRG/MSOL, the lack of empirical or experimental comparisons on the cited datasets (AMR, Prague, UCCA) means key baselines and fair evaluation conditions are missing. This yields inadequate empirical comparison despite good citation of prior work.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents no empirical baselines ("Baselines compared: none mentioned") and provides no evaluation metrics, so it lacks meaningful empirical comparisons against appropriate recent methods despite using datasets like AMR and UCCA. It does, however, give thorough related-work coverage and theoretical comparisons (e.g., RGL as a subfamily of HRL/MSOL and complexity/expressivity tradeoffs), but the absence of empirical baselines or fair experimental evaluation makes the comparison effort inadequate.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper reports no baseline comparisons (Baselines compared: none mentioned) and provides no evaluation metrics, so it fails to empirically compare the proposed RGG parsing approach against recent methods. While the related work coverage is reported as thorough and the paper discusses theoretical relationships to HRG/MSOL, the lack of empirical or experimental comparisons on the cited datasets (AMR, Prague, UCCA) means key baselines and fair evaluation conditions are missing. This yields inadequate empirical comparison despite good citation of prior work.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important problem—efficient, formally grounded parsing and probabilistic modeling for graph languages—by defining Regular Graph Grammars (RGG) with constraints C1/C2 and proving closure under intersection and MSOL membership. The provision of an Earley-like top-down parser with linear-time runtime in input graph size is likely to change how researchers design and implement grammar-based graph parsers and enables practical probabilistic extensions. While the C1/C2 constraints and noted expressivity limitations mean the approach may not cover all use cases (and some grammars become unnatural), the work opens clear new directions for probabilistic regular graph languages and efficient parsing for semantic graph formalisms (e.g., AMR, UCCA). Overall, this is a strong-impact contribution for the graph-grammar and semantic parsing subfields, though its broader applicability may be somewhat constrained. | B(4): The paper tackles an important practical/theoretical problem by identifying Regular Graph Grammars (RGG) as a MSOL/HRG-subfamily and proving closure under intersection while providing an Earley-like top-down parser with linear-time complexity—advances that could materially improve graph parsing for AMR/UCCA and enable probabilistic RGLs. The constraints C1/C2 that make RGGs MSOL-members and support efficient parsing are practically useful, but the authors note these constraints can make some grammars unnatural and may limit expressivity, and the parser assumes an identifiable external node. Given these trade-offs, the work is likely to strongly influence research on efficient and probabilistic graph grammars, though adoption may hinge on how restrictive the constraints prove in practice.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important problem—efficient, formally grounded parsing and probabilistic modeling for graph languages—by defining Regular Graph Grammars (RGG) with constraints C1/C2 and proving closure under intersection and MSOL membership. The provision of an Earley-like top-down parser with linear-time runtime in input graph size is likely to change how researchers design and implement grammar-based graph parsers and enables practical probabilistic extensions. While the C1/C2 constraints and noted expressivity limitations mean the approach may not cover all use cases (and some grammars become unnatural), the work opens clear new directions for probabilistic regular graph languages and efficient parsing for semantic graph formalisms (e.g., AMR, UCCA). Overall, this is a strong-impact contribution for the graph-grammar and semantic parsing subfields, though its broader applicability may be somewhat constrained.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important practical/theoretical problem by identifying Regular Graph Grammars (RGG) as a MSOL/HRG-subfamily and proving closure under intersection while providing an Earley-like top-down parser with linear-time complexity—advances that could materially improve graph parsing for AMR/UCCA and enable probabilistic RGLs. The constraints C1/C2 that make RGGs MSOL-members and support efficient parsing are practically useful, but the authors note these constraints can make some grammars unnatural and may limit expressivity, and the parser assumes an identifiable external node. Given these trade-offs, the work is likely to strongly influence research on efficient and probabilistic graph grammars, though adoption may hinge on how restrictive the constraints prove in practice.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper presents explicit theoretical results (proofs of closure under intersection and a top-down Earley-like parser with claimed linear runtime) and defines RGGs with constraints C1/C2 to enable MSOL membership, which supports the main formal claims. However, based only on the summary I cannot verify the correctness or completeness of the proofs, and the introduced constraints plus the assumption of an identifiable external node are notable caveats that may limit applicability. Empirical evaluation is weak: datasets are listed but no baselines, metrics, or code are reported, so experimental support is insufficient. Overall, the theoretical contributions appear plausible but some technical and empirical gaps warrant caution. | B(4): The summary states the main technical claims (RGL ⊆ HRL and MSOL, closure under intersection, and an Earley-like top-down parser with a proven linear runtime) and indicates formal proofs are provided for closure and complexity, which supports the core theoretical contributions. However, there are notable technical caveats: the C1/C2 constraints that enable these results may substantially restrict expressivity (the authors themselves call some grammars "unnatural"), and the parser requires an identifiable external marker node — a nontrivial assumption for some inputs. Experimental methodology is weak or absent (no baselines, no metrics), so empirical support for practicality is lacking. These are minor-to-moderate issues that do not invalidate the theoretical results but reduce confidence in general applicability.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents explicit theoretical results (proofs of closure under intersection and a top-down Earley-like parser with claimed linear runtime) and defines RGGs with constraints C1/C2 to enable MSOL membership, which supports the main formal claims. However, based only on the summary I cannot verify the correctness or completeness of the proofs, and the introduced constraints plus the assumption of an identifiable external node are notable caveats that may limit applicability. Empirical evaluation is weak: datasets are listed but no baselines, metrics, or code are reported, so experimental support is insufficient. Overall, the theoretical contributions appear plausible but some technical and empirical gaps warrant caution.

reviewer_b.justification: The summary states the main technical claims (RGL ⊆ HRL and MSOL, closure under intersection, and an Earley-like top-down parser with a proven linear runtime) and indicates formal proofs are provided for closure and complexity, which supports the core theoretical contributions. However, there are notable technical caveats: the C1/C2 constraints that enable these results may substantially restrict expressivity (the authors themselves call some grammars "unnatural"), and the parser requires an identifiable external marker node — a nontrivial assumption for some inputs. Experimental methodology is weak or absent (no baselines, no metrics), so empirical support for practicality is lacking. These are minor-to-moderate issues that do not invalidate the theoretical results but reduce confidence in general applicability.

main_claims: Regular graph languages (RGLs) are a subfamily of both hyperedge replacement languages (HRL) and monadic second-order languages (MSOL), thus inheriting the ability to be made probabilistic and closure under intersection.
We prove that RGLs are closed under intersection.
We provide an efficient top-down parsing algorithm for regular graph grammars (RGG) that generalizes Earley’s algorithm and has runtime linear in the size of the input graph.
We define Regular Graph Grammars (RGG) with constraints (C1 and C2) that enable membership in MSOL and more efficient parsing than general HRG.
_summary.methodology: Define Regular Graph Grammars (RGG) as restricted hyperedge replacement grammars satisfying constraints C1 and C2; impose a fixed order on edges of right-hand sides and use boundary representations to design a top-down, Earley-like parser and prove closure and complexity bounds (linear-time parsing).
limitations: RGG may be too constrained and not expressive enough for some natural derivations (the grammar in Table 1 is described as unnatural and C2 makes certain derivation orders difficult to express).
The parsing approach assumes an identifiable external (marker) node in the input graph (a mild assumption noted for some applications).
